claims.claim_text: These 9 commands cover about 80% of everyday terminal work.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By the end of this module, you'll know 9 core commands that cover many everyday terminal tasks.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage reads like an unsupported statistic, but the broader teaching point that a small set of commands goes a long way is sound.

claims.claim_text: `rm` removes files immediately, usually without an "Are you sure?" prompt or built-in undo, and tools such as `shred` are used when stronger recovery resistance is needed.
claims.span_hint: section: Command 8 / WARNING: There Is No Recycle Bin
claims.rationale: GNU coreutils documentation directly covers `rm` prompting behavior, recoverability caveats, and `shred`, but `gnu.org` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Deleted files may sometimes be recoverable before their data is overwritten.
claims.span_hint: section: Command 8 / WARNING: There Is No Recycle Bin
claims.rationale: NIST directly documents deleted-file recovery as a real forensic capability.

claims.claim_text: In 1998, Toy Story 2 was nearly wiped by an `rm` command, about 90% of the film's files were lost, and the project survived because a technical director had a backup at home.
claims.rationale: This named incident appears in an official Disney/Pixar video, but `video.disney.com` is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: On modern GNU/Linux systems using GNU `rm`, `rm -rf /` is normally blocked by the default `--preserve-root` safety guard unless that protection is explicitly disabled.
claims.rationale: The GNU `rm` manual directly documents `--preserve-root` as the default and `--no-preserve-root` as the override, but `gnu.org` is off-list.

claims.claim_text: Command-line interfaces became common in the 1960s and remained the dominant way users interacted with software for decades.
claims.rationale: The cited history page directly places CLI emergence in the mid-1960s and describes it as the most common interface for roughly three decades.

claims.claim_text: The Apple Macintosh, released in 1984, is credited with popularizing the graphical user interface.
claims.rationale: The source directly states that the Macintosh system popularized the GUI concept and dates the original Macintosh release to 1984.

claims.claim_text: CTSS had a `listf` file-listing command in 1961.
claims.rationale: The CTSS history page explicitly lists `listf` among the early console commands in the 1961 system.

claims.claim_text: The modern `ls` command appeared in the first version of Unix.
claims.rationale: The `ls` article directly states that an `ls` command appeared in the first version of AT&T Unix.

claims.claim_text: Unix home-directory shorthand with `~` traces back to the Lear-Siegler ADM-3A terminal, where the tilde key was labeled Home.
claims.rationale: The source directly explains the home-directory convention as deriving from the ADM-3A key labeling.

further_reading.title: Command-line interface
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides beginner-friendly historical context for terminals, shells, and why command lines still matter.

further_reading.title: Deleted File Recovery
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds the module's warning that deletion and permanent erasure are not the same thing.

further_reading.title: ls
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives historical and behavioral context for one of the core commands introduced in the module.

notes: Quiz items, exercise steps, code blocks, tables, and teaching analogies were intentionally excluded. Two off-allowlist domains surfaced for primary support: gnu.org for GNU coreutils command semantics and video.disney.com for the Toy Story 2 incident clip.
_schema_issues: claim[0]:hard_cite_class_cannot_soften:statistic/soften_to_illustration — must be supported, weak_anchor, or needs_allowlist_expansion
